caveats: a: The occupation source includes mining support outside oil and gas.
a: The code's service-line mix makes one exposure range imprecise.
rho: Large-vendor digital capabilities may not be affordable or transferable to LMM firms.
rho: No direct five-year implementation survey exists.
e: Establishment counts exceed firm counts.
e: The injected n uses one sector margin across service lines with very different capital intensity and profitability.
s5: Owner age is not observed transaction flow.
s5: Private deals and small-company failures are incompletely reported.
q: Commercial structures differ sharply by service line and basin.
q: Public-company contracts may not represent LMM bargaining power.
d5: The service basket is heterogeneous and no single activity index covers it.
d5: Orphan-well funding supports only one segment and is time-limited.
d5: Producing-well count does not reveal annual outsourced service frequency.
o: Some logging interpretation and advisory work may become software-delivered faster than physical services.
o: Operator consolidation can reduce independent share without eliminating the work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat outsourced field jobs for E&P operators under master service agreements, pricing schedules, per-stage or per-job quotes, daywork/hourly terms, and occasional longer contracts; activity and price depend on well completions, producing-well maintenance, decommissioning, customer budgets, and service capacity.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 213112 spans data/advisory exploration and many materially different field services from high-capital fracturing to low-capital well cleaning and plugging. For business-model coherence, the lens keeps recurring crew-and-equipment field operators across the well lifecycle and excludes pure advisory exploration, product-only sales, and passive rental. The narrowing is based on operating model, not perceived attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure geological or exploration advisory firms without recurring crew-and-equipment field operations; product-only sellers; passive equipment lessors; captive service units; contract drilling classified in 213111; geophysical surveying, site construction, pipeline work, and transportation classified elsewhere; firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing crew-and-equipment field services to external oil and gas operators across completion, intervention, production support, and end-of-life work, including hydraulic fracturing, cementing, wireline, well logging/testing, casing and tubing work, chemical treatment, cleanout, swabbing, workover, equipment servicing, and plugging.

a.bridge: BLS measures all NAICS 213100 support activities for mining rather than 213112 or the narrowed field-service lens and does not measure AI exposure. I assign partial exposure to job design, logging interpretation, quoting, dispatch, maintenance planning, HSE documentation, reporting, inventory, billing, and customer support, while assigning little exposure to rig-up, pressure pumping, wireline handling, workover, repair, and plugging. Broader mining support may understate data-heavy oilfield services but may overstate office work relative to small field crews.
caveats: The occupation source includes mining support outside oil and gas.
The code's service-line mix makes one exposure range imprecise.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 213112 SOC roster split by service line and time studies covering job design, logging, dispatch, maintenance, HSE, reporting, billing, and field execution.
a.rationale: The broader occupation mix is 46.43% construction/extraction and 9.16% installation/maintenance/repair, with substantial roustabout and supervisory employment. AI opportunity is therefore concentrated in technical interpretation and field-service coordination rather than the majority of hands-on work.

d5.bridge: EIA rig counts and production outlooks measure upstream activity, the producing-well count measures installed wells rather than service events, and DOI grants measure a funded plugging niche. I translate these into the selected field-service basket: completions track new wells, intervention follows a large aging stock, and plugging adds countercyclical demand. Service intensity, pricing, and contractor share can diverge, so the proxy can err in either direction.
caveats: The service basket is heterogeneous and no single activity index covers it.
Orphan-well funding supports only one segment and is time-limited.
Producing-well count does not reveal annual outsourced service frequency.
d5.change_evidence: Basin-level completion and frac-stage forecasts, active workover/wireline units, producing-well intervention frequency, service pricing/utilization, E&P budgets, and awarded plugging backlogs through 2031.
d5.rationale: US rotary rigs averaged 553 in May 2026, while a current public provider reported activity declines and pricing pressure in 2025. EIA counted 918,481 producing wells in 2024, 78% producing 15 BOE/d or less, sustaining maintenance and intervention needs even if drilling slows. DOI's state orphan-well program provides about $4.2 billion and reported more than 10,000 wells plugged through mid-2025. Near-term oil/gas output remains high, but longer-term oil forecasts and service productivity constrain the base.

caveats: Establishment counts exceed firm counts.
The injected n uses one sector margin across service lines with very different capital intensity and profitability.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 213112 establishment-to-owner map with service-line classification, active crews/equipment, US external revenue, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and captive/product/rental flags.
e.rationale: The industry is contract/fee support by definition and Census reports 9,627 employer establishments, including 4,917 with fewer than five employees and 3,783 with 5–49 employees. Many regional field-service firms can fit the band, but very small shops, inactive operators, product/rental-heavy firms, pure advisory businesses removed by the lens, subsidiaries, and entities with out-of-band through-cycle EBITDA reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 213112 ($24,411,449,389) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 213112 ($78,203,637,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0776 (margins.json[213]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Oilfield Svcs/Equip. EBITDA/Sales 7.76%); revenue/employee $409,963 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 213112); class <5: 4,398 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,317 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,042 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,987,399 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $154,222 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 823 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,760,611 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $291,823 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,094 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,136,965 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,097,029 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 253 firms, avg revenue/firm $69,557,802 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,397,685 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 121 firms, avg revenue/firm $310,072,917 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,061,658 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,347

caveats: Some logging interpretation and advisory work may become software-delivered faster than physical services.
Operator consolidation can reduce independent share without eliminating the work.
o.change_evidence: Observed autonomous field-service fleets, remotely operated intervention systems with materially reduced contractor responsibility, or sustained E&P insourcing that eliminates independent service operators across multiple service lines.
o.rationale: Completion, intervention, pressure control, equipment handling, maintenance, and plugging remain physical, hazardous work requiring accountable contractors with trained crews and specialized equipment. Remote operations and AI can reduce headcount and interpretation work, while E&P consolidation or insourcing can displace vendors, but software alone cannot deliver the service basket in five years.

q.bridge: Nine Energy's filing describes competitive job pricing for a public wireline and cementing provider, while ProFrac describes completion-service terms; neither measures AI-benefit retention in LMM firms or all selected service lines. I infer that short, spot-priced jobs reprice gains quickly, whereas MSAs and scarce technical niches preserve some benefit. Large-company procurement may understate retention for differentiated local specialists but overstate it where small vendors lack scale.
caveats: Commercial structures differ sharply by service line and basin.
Public-company contracts may not represent LMM bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: Representative LMM MSAs, price books, statements of work, bids, escalation clauses, performance bonuses, rebid histories, and gross-margin bridges following documented automation.
q.rationale: Specific jobs are competitively bid or negotiated, with rates set by location, complexity, conditions, duration, and markets; most services are spot-like and each job stands alone. Completion services also use per-job, turnkey, daywork, and hourly terms. Efficiency gains can improve job margin and utilization, but customer procurement and excess capacity drive pass-through at the next quote.

caveats: Large-vendor digital capabilities may not be affordable or transferable to LMM firms.
No direct five-year implementation survey exists.
rho.change_evidence: Service-line-specific deployments from independent operators showing adoption, labor hours, job-cycle time, interpretation accuracy, equipment downtime, safety events, billing leakage, and integration cost over multiple customers.
rho.rationale: Logging, job design, dispatch, maintenance, and reporting have usable digital data and can see meaningful implementation within five years. Fragmented small-firm systems, remote connectivity, customer-specific workflows, equipment integration, cybersecurity, liability, and high-pressure safety requirements keep humans accountable and limit autonomous execution.

s5.bridge: Census ABS owner-age tables cover all employer businesses, not eligible oilfield-service firms or control transfers. I use owner aging as succession pressure and discount for internal succession, closures, bankruptcy, asset-only sales, corporate subsidiaries, and commodity-cycle timing. The large population of small establishments may make succession more relevant than in drilling, but distressed exits can make the proxy overstate transferable operations.
caveats: Owner age is not observed transaction flow.
Private deals and small-company failures are incompletely reported.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of Enverus, PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, bankruptcy, and auction records matched to eligible firms and tagged as whole-company control, asset-only sale, closure, or internal succession.
s5.rationale: Regional service firms often depend on founders and customer relationships, while consolidation creates strategic buyers. Cyclicality, equipment condition, environmental liabilities, crew retention, and customer concentration cause many exits to be asset sales or closures rather than qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: A large outsourced service base and data-rich coordination around hazardous field work create meaningful bounded automation potential.
principal_weakness: Extreme service-line heterogeneity and spot-market pricing weaken both a single demand estimate and long-run benefit retention.

sources.supports: Current industry definition and 9,627 employer establishments in 2023 CBP.

sources.supports: Broader occupation mix: construction/extraction 46.43%, roustabouts 11.92%, and installation/maintenance/repair 9.16%.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Support Activities for Mining

sources.supports: Documents vehicle, struck-by, explosion, fall, high-pressure, electrical, and machinery hazards and the need for training and job safety analysis.

sources.supports: Reports 4,917 establishments with fewer than five employees, 1,397 with 5–9, 1,253 with 10–19, and 1,133 with 20–49.

sources.supports: Reports 918,481 producing wells in 2024 and 78% producing 15 BOE/d or less in 2023–2024.

sources.supports: Describes physical remedial operations including sand cleanout, casing repair, recompletion, sidetracking, and plug-back.
sources.title: OSHA Oil and Gas Well Servicing: Workover

sources.supports: Documents digital and AI solutions alongside logging, cement integrity, well intervention, completion, fluids, and project-management services; supports technical availability but not LMM implementation.
sources.title: Halliburton 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Official Census Age of Owner tables for employer businesses, used only as a broad succession proxy.
sources.title: ABS Characteristics of Business Owners: 2024 Tables

sources.supports: States jobs are competitively bid or negotiated, most business is spot-like, and 2025 brought activity declines and pricing pressure across service lines.
sources.title: Nine Energy Service 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Describes per-job hydraulic-fracturing pricing and turnkey, daywork, or hourly terms for wireline and cementing; demand depends on prices, completion budgets, and rig count.
sources.title: ProFrac Holding 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Program provides about $4.2 billion to states for plugging, remediation, characterization, reclamation, and associated infrastructure removal.
sources.title: Department of the Interior State Orphaned Wells Program

sources.supports: US rotary rigs averaged 553 in May 2026, including 535 onshore and 18 offshore.
sources.title: EIA Crude Oil and Natural Gas Drilling Activity
